era_context: Egypt's Old Kingdom, Dynasty IV. The reign of Khafre (c. 2558–2532 BCE according to conventional chronology, though this dating is uncertain). Khafre's reign saw the construction of the second pyramid at Giza and the Great Sphinx. Mesopotamia was in the Early Dynastic III period. The Indus Valley Civilisation was in its early urban phase. This year represents the continuation of the pyramid-building age under a new pharaoh.

events.title: Reign of Khafre; construction of Khafre's pyramid and the Great Sphinx
events.description: Khafre's reign (c. 2558–2532 BCE according to conventional chronology) saw the construction of the second pyramid at Giza and the Great Sphinx. The Sphinx, carved from a single limestone outcrop, is one of the world's largest monolithic statues and represents a lion with a human head (traditionally identified as Khafre). The construction of these monuments continued the Dynasty IV tradition of pharaonic monumentality and the mobilisation of vast labour forces. Khafre's reign represents the continuation and refinement of the architectural and administrative systems established under Khufu.

sources.name: Khafre's pyramid and the Great Sphinx (archaeological evidence)

events.certainty_note: Khafre's reign is conventionally dated to c. 2558–2532 BCE, placing 2565 BCE within his reign. However, this dating is based on king-list regnal lengths and architectural analysis, both of which have inherent uncertainty. Alternative chronologies shift this date by 50–100 years. The exact year-to-year events of his reign are not documented.

events.title: Construction or continuation of the Great Sphinx of Giza
events.description: The Great Sphinx of Giza is traditionally attributed to Khafre's reign. The Sphinx is a monumental limestone statue, approximately 240 feet (73 metres) long and 66 feet (20 metres) high, carved from a single limestone outcrop. It depicts a lion with a human head, traditionally identified as Khafre. The Sphinx's construction required sophisticated quarrying, carving, and engineering techniques. Its purpose is debated: it may have served as a guardian figure for the pyramid complex, a representation of royal power, or a religious symbol. The Sphinx's exact dating is contested; some scholars argue for a later date (New Kingdom or later), but the conventional attribution to Khafre remains dominant.

sources.name: Dream Stela of Thutmose IV (New Kingdom inscription mentioning the Sphinx)

events.certainty_note: The attribution to Khafre is traditional and based on stylistic analysis and proximity to Khafre's pyramid. However, the Sphinx's exact dating is contested. Some scholars argue for a much later date (New Kingdom or even later), based on geological and archaeological arguments. The conventional attribution to Khafre remains dominant in Egyptology, but is not definitively proven.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Khafre's reign and the construction of the Great Sphinx is contested. The conventional chronology places Khafre c. 2558–2532 BCE, but alternative chronologies shift this date by 50–100 years. The attribution of the Sphinx to Khafre is traditional but not definitively proven. Some scholars, notably John Anthony West and Robert Bauval, have argued that the Sphinx is much older than the conventional dating, possibly dating to the Old Kingdom but carved at a different time than Khafre's reign. Geological arguments about water erosion have been used to support this claim, though mainstream Egyptology rejects these arguments. The Sphinx's purpose and symbolism are also debated; it may not have been a 'guardian' figure but rather a representation of royal power or a religious symbol.
historiographic_note: The historical record for this year is almost entirely archaeological. No contemporary written records survive. The bias toward monumental architecture obscures the broader social, economic, and demographic context. Chronological uncertainty of ±50–100 years is inherent. The attribution of monuments to specific pharaohs is based on stylistic analysis, proximity, and later king lists, all of which are subject to error. The Sphinx's dating is particularly contested, with some scholars arguing for a much older or younger date than the conventional attribution.

graph_edges.note: Construction of Khafre's pyramid and the Great Sphinx during Khafre's reign
Khafre's reign follows Khufu's; continuation of Dynasty IV pyramid-building
